ground_truth.title: Food Prices for Nutrition – Diet cost metrics for a better fed world
ground_truth.main_content: Tufts University research on Food Prices for Nutrition shows how the cost and affordability of healthy diets can be used to monitor food access and guide intervention. Our methods start with data on availability and price that is routinely collected around the world. We match item descriptions to food composition, and measure access to healthy diets using the least expensive locally available items that would meet national guidelines and global targets. Our mission is to equip governments and civil society with accurate, updated metrics to monitor the cost and affordability of healthy diets, for use in guiding agriculture and food system change. We envision a global food system that brings healthy diets within reach of all people at all times. Food Prices for Nutrition work led by Tufts University developed the methods behind FAO and World Bank estimates that about 2.6 billion people in 2024 could not afford a healthy diet. Our metrics help identify the causes of poor diet quality by distinguishing among three possible causes: unusually high prices for the least expensive items needed from each food group, inadequate incomes to buy sufficient quantities of the lowest-cost items needed for a healthy diet, and any displacement of foods in a low-cost healthy diet by other items for reasons other than price and nutritional value. The diet cost metrics are based on identifying the least expensive locally available foods in sufficient quantities to meet a population's nutritional needs. Results shown in our figures reveal some variance in the Cost of a Healthy Diet but no correlation with national income, while actual food spending is generally higher at higher income levels. This diagnostic metric helps identify locally-appropriate interventions to improve access through innovations and investment in food supply to lower cost, safety nets and livelihoods to raise income, or other actions to alter food choice from among the affordable options, and thereby achieve universal access and use of healthy diets worldwide. The project involves collaborators from Tufts University, the World Bank, and IFPRI working across multiple countries including Bangladesh, India, Pakistan, Burkina Faso, Ethiopia, Ghana, Malawi, Nigeria, and Tanzania.
with: Food Prices for Nutrition work led by Tufts University developed the methods behind FAO and World Bank estimates that about 2.6 billion people in 2024 could not afford a healthy diet.
Our metrics help identify the causes of poor diet quality by distinguishing among three possible causes: unusually high prices, inadequate incomes, and displacement of foods by other items.
When healthy diets are unaffordable, food prices relative to available income are an insurmountable barrier to reaching international standards of diet quality.
Results shown above reveal some variance in the Cost of a Healthy Diet but no correlation with national income, while actual food spending is generally higher at higher income levels.
This diagnostic metric helps identify locally-appropriate interventions to improve access through innovations and investment in food supply to lower cost, safety nets and livelihoods to raise income.
